rationale: Clinical SOAP notes require deep medical terminology comprehension, ICD-10 code inference from narrative descriptions, structured extraction from free-form prose sections, and clinical reasoning to classify diagnosis status (Active/Chronic/Resolved/New/Suspected). Claude Sonnet excels at understanding nuanced clinical language, correctly mapping informal clinical shorthand to structured fields (e.g., 'HTN' to Essential hypertension, 'DM2' to Type 2 diabetes), and producing deterministic zero-temperature extractions essential for clinical data integrity. Temperature is set to 0.0 to eliminate hallucination risk — critical when generating ICD-10 codes that feed billing and quality programs. DBRX Instruct serves as fallback for well-structured EHR-exported notes with discrete labeled sections where medical reasoning depth is less critical.
